nutrient_source.title: Cho Cho Chocolate — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo database entry for Cho Cho Chocolate (cr_id G00010463). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — chocolate milk, low-fat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cho Cho Chocolate is a dilute chocolate milk beverage (42 kcal/100g, 10.36g carbohydrate/100g). GI of 43 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for low-fat chocolate milk (GI ~43, Atkinson et al. 2008). This is the closest defensible analogous food from an approved GI source. Glycemic load per 250g serving = (43 × 10.36 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 11.1, rounded to 11.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.94g/100g) with negligible dietary fibre (0g) and very low potassium (5mg/100g); added sugar independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Cho Cho Chocolate to occasional small portions (≤125 ml) due to its high sugar-to-fibre ratio and negligible potassium; the AHA recommends limiting added sugar to ≤25g/day for women and ≤36g/day for men, and a 250 ml serving provides approximately 24.9g sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 9.94g/100g (approximately 24.9g per 250 ml serving) with 0g dietary fibre provides no glycaemic modulation; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Cho Cho Chocolate or limit intake to no more than 100 ml on rare occasions, pairing with a protein source to blunt the glycaemic response; the absence of dietary fibre means there is no buffer against rapid glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free-sugar content (9.94g/100g) with negligible fibre, potassium, and micronutrients; regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat Cho Cho Chocolate as an occasional treat only, limiting intake to ≤125 ml per occasion, as habitual sugar-sweetened beverage consumption is linked to dyslipidaemia, weight gain, and elevated triglycerides per AHA 2018 dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cho Cho Chocolate is a commercially prepared, ready-to-drink chocolate milk beverage sold in Singapore, commonly available in 250 ml cartons in schools, convenience stores, and supermarkets. It is a dilute, sweet chocolate drink made from a blend of water, sugar, cocoa powder, and milk solids, delivering a mild chocolate flavour with a light, watery texture rather than the thick creaminess of full-fat chocolate milk. Per 100g, it provides 42 kcal, 0g protein, 0.25g fat, and 10.36g carbohydrate of which 9.94g is sugar, with negligible dietary fibre (0g), very low sodium (3mg), and minimal micronutrients. The low caloric density reflects its high water content and dilute formulation. Clinically, the product is a sugar-sweetened beverage with a high sugar-to-fibre ratio, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. The GI is estimated at approximately 43 (low GI category) based on analogous low-fat chocolate milk data from the University of Sydney GI Database; however, the glycaemic load per 250 ml serving is approximately 11, which is clinically meaningful. The product is classified as ultra-processed and contains milk allergens. It is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy intolerance.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the canonical HPB Foodo database entry (cr_id G00010463) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from the closest approved analogous food (low-fat chocolate milk, University of Sydney GI Database). All values are internally consistent with the product description.
notes: protein_g is reported as 0.0 per the HPB Foodo canonical record (cr_id G00010463); this appears to reflect the formulation of this specific dilute chocolate beverage product where milk solids contribute negligible protein at the reported dilution level — confirmed zero per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 per HPB source — confirmed zero; this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-contributing ingredients. magnesium_mg is 0.0 per HPB source — confirmed zero at the reported dilution. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 per HPB source — confirmed zero by formulation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific product are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Grains and staples / Baking ingredients' (input) to 'Beverages / Chocolate milk drinks' based on the product description and nutritional profile, which is consistent with a ready-to-drink chocolate beverage. GI of 43 is an analogous estimate based on low-fat chocolate milk from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI measurement for Cho Cho Chocolate was found in approved sources.
